{{< pagebreak >}}

# Day 4 — Choose one small shift {#day4}

```{=typst}
#wcg-lead[Today you turn one observation into a small, specific plan.]
#wcg-visual(height: 1.3in, label: [A path making one small, calm turn])
```

## Why this matters

Big resolutions often collapse, and vague intentions fade. Research suggests a
simple fix: a specific "when-then" plan — deciding in advance what you will do in a
certain situation — is linked with better follow-through than a general
intention,^8^ with a medium-to-large average effect across many studies.^9^

The key word is *specific*. "Spend less" is a wish. "When I open the shopping app
after dinner, then I'll add items to a list" is a plan you can picture.

## An everyday example

A when-then plan might be: "When I feel the urge to buy something at night, then
I'll add it to a list and look again tomorrow." Small, clear, and easy to follow —
which is exactly what makes it work.

```{=typst}
#wcg-action[
  Write one when-then shift based on something you noticed this week. Keep it
  small enough that it feels almost too easy. Small is the point.
]
```

> **Notice.**
>
> What is one small, realistic change I could try?

You are choosing one shift, worded so clearly that your future self knows exactly
what to do.

{{< include assets/worksheets/ws-4-small-shift.qmd >}}
